5 min read
Just now

--

Last Friday, Apple filed a 41-page lawsuit accusing OpenAI of trade secret theft. This is a federal complaint in the Northern District of California
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In 2024, Apple and OpenAI stood on a WWDC stage and got married. Tim Cook put ChatGPT inside the iPhone, Sam Altman watched from the crowd looking very pleased with himself, and together they tried to rebrand “artificial intelligence” as “Apple Intelligence,” a marketing campaign so convincing it personally inspired at least one person I know to switch to Android out of spite.
Two years later, the marriage is over, and it did not end quietly. Last Friday, Apple filed a 41-page lawsuit accusing OpenAI of trade secret theft “at every level,” calling its hardware business rotten at the core. This isn’t a subtweet. This is a federal complaint in the Northern District of California, and it reads less like a tech dispute and more like a corporate spy thriller nobody bothered to make boring.
Act 1: OpenAI decided it wants to eat the Apple, not date it
Here’s the part that actually explains the timing. OpenAI has quietly poached something like 400 Apple employees, and more relevantly, spent roughly $6.5 billion buying io, the hardware startup co-founded by Jony Ive, the guy who spent close to 30 years designing the actual visual language of the iPhone alongside Steve Jobs. Ive now runs OpenAI’s device team. Do the math on that acquisition price against the startup’s headcount and you’re looking at something like $3 billion per employee, which is either a genius long-term bet or the most expensive way in history to say “we wanted the guy, not the company.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

And what is OpenAI actually building with him? Leaks this week describe a screen-free, moveable smart speaker running on GPT-Live, OpenAI’s real-time voice engine, one that’s explicitly being designed to feel “alive,” with mechanical parts that move on their own so it reads less like an appliance and more like a pet that occasionally answers your emails. Reported price: $200 to $300. Reported release: 2027. Reported vibe: an Echo that developed a personality and immediately started oversharing.
I’ll withhold judgment on whether a fidgety AI speaker with main character energy is going to out-compete the iPhone. Apple, apparently, is not withholding judgment on how OpenAI got there.
Act 2: the recruiting process allegedly doubled as a heist
This is where the complaint stops sounding like a business dispute and starts sounding unhinged. Apple’s suit names OpenAI’s hardware chief, Tang Tan, a former Apple VP, and accuses him of using Apple’s internal project codenames during interviews to pump candidates for more detail, and allegedly telling candidates to bring actual Apple hardware components to their interviews for “show and tell.” Most technical interviews ask you to reverse a linked list. Apparently OpenAI’s version wanted you to bring the linked list. Physically. Still under NDA.
Apple also claims Tan circulated internal guidance coaching departing Apple employees on how to dodge the standard security walkout, the process where security walks you out the building the second you resign so you can’t swing by your desk and grab anything interesting on the way out.
Act 3: meet the actual worst spy in recent corporate history
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Then there’s Chang Liu, a senior electrical engineer who left Apple for OpenAI, allegedly kept a company laptop, and used it to dig around Apple’s internal network. According to the complaint, he did this while texting a colleague on an unencrypted channel. That’s the corporate espionage equivalent of committing a crime directly in front of a security camera and then waving at it. He reportedly said he could still get into internal storage and thought it was funny. Hours after officially quitting, he allegedly mentioned he still had a second company laptop he planned to keep using.
He got caught because Apple found the messages sitting on the company-owned, company-monitored device he used to send them. If there’s a lesson here it’s that “10x engineer” and “competent criminal” are not the same skill tree, and nobody on this deal seems to have picked up the second one.
The part where Apple’s outrage gets a little ironic
I don’t feel too bad for Apple here, and not because the allegations aren’t serious. If true, they’re genuinely bad. It’s more that Apple’s entire origin story runs through Xerox PARC, where Steve Jobs toured the building, saw the GUI and the mouse, and had his engineers go build Apple’s own version shortly after. He liked a certain line attributed to Picasso, something about how good artists copy and great artists steal. The tech industry’s worst-kept secret is that borrowing your way to the frontier is basically the founding tradition. Apple just prefers being the one doing the borrowing.
Why this actually matters beyond the drama
Strip out the theatrics and there’s a real story underneath. OpenAI is racing toward what’s expected to be a massive IPO, and a federal lawsuit alleging systematic trade secret theft, with named executives, alleged internal cheat sheets, and a hardware business the plaintiff is publicly calling “rotten to its core,” is exactly the kind of headline that makes due diligence lawyers nervous. Apple isn’t just suing over stolen files. It’s throwing sand in the gears of OpenAI’s hardware timeline right as the company needs its story to look clean for investors.
Whether the screenless speaker with feelings ships on schedule now depends less on engineering and more on how this plays out in a California courtroom.
Anyone else think the real twist here isn’t the theft allegations, it’s that OpenAI’s answer to the iPhone is a fidgety smart speaker? Curious what you’d actually want that thing to do before you’d buy one. Drop it below.
